coredata.dc:title: Novel metal-free in-plane functionalized graphitic carbon nitride with graphene quantum dots for effective photodegradation of 4-bromophenol
coredata.dc:creator: Shanker Sahu, Rama
Dubey, Abhishek
Shih, Yang-hsin
coredata.dc:description: The current study reveals the in-plane induction of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into exfoliated graphitic carbon nitride to generate greener material for visible light driven photodegradation of 4–bromophenol, a novel brominated flame retardant. The physical and electrochemical analysis shows the GQDs induction within the exfoliated carbon nitride (CN-Ex), generating new impurities, thus exploiting higher visible–light. The CN-Ex has exceptional conductivity to convert electrons from valence band, while GQDs generate an electron field to enhance the catalytic activity on conduction band. Metal-free CN@GQD composite has shown efficient and consistent photodegradation rate of 4-BP under visible light, which could be debrominated and oxidized separately on conduction and valence band, respectively. EPR spectra reveals that 1O2 radicals prominently dominates the photodegradation of 4-BP. TOC removal and the rate kinetics of 4-BP was insignificantly reduced after consecutive 5-repeated cycles. Moreover, excellent stability and durability was observed under various reaction conditions to confirm the feasibility and broader applicability of the material in the industries. This research reveals the inclusion of GQDs within CN layers could modify the band structure, enhance hydrophilicity and photocatalytic efficacy, unveiling an ingenuity to architect an effective and visible-light driven metal-free photocatalyst for persistent NBFRs degradation.

head.ce:title: Novel metal-free in-plane functionalized graphitic carbon nitride with graphene quantum dots for effective photodegradation of 4-bromophenol

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study reveals the in-plane induction of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into exfoliated graphitic carbon nitride to generate greener material for visible light driven photodegradation of 4–bromophenol, a novel brominated flame retardant. The physical and electrochemical analysis shows the GQDs induction within the exfoliated carbon nitride (CN-Ex), generating new impurities, thus exploiting higher visible–light. The CN-Ex has exceptional conductivity to convert electrons from valence band, while GQDs generate an electron field to enhance the catalytic activity on conduction band. Metal-free CN@GQD composite has shown efficient and consistent photodegradation rate of 4-BP under visible light, which could be debrominated and oxidized separately on conduction and valence band, respectively. EPR spectra reveals that 1O2 radicals prominently dominates the photodegradation of 4-BP. TOC removal and the rate kinetics of 4-BP was insignificantly reduced after consecutive 5-repeated cycles. Moreover, excellent stability and durability was observed under various reaction conditions to confirm the feasibility and broader applicability of the material in the industries. This research reveals the inclusion of GQDs within CN layers could modify the band structure, enhance hydrophilicity and photocatalytic efficacy, unveiling an ingenuity to architect an effective and visible-light driven metal-free photocatalyst for persistent NBFRs degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Halophenols are obstinate organic substance, widely existing in the environment, discharged from numerous industrial processes including, brominated flame retardants (BFRs), polymer intermediates, drilling fluid, pharmaceuticals, and wood preservatives [1]. Since the ban on BFRs by the United Nations Stockholm Convention in 2013 [2], has been eliminated in many countries in the world by 2016 except China [3], novel BFR (NBFR) has emerged. Zuiderveen et al. [4] summarized the information of 63 NBFRs from European Chemicals Agency, including hypothetical toxicity and persistence in the environment. Above all, brominated phenols (BPs) are toxic and persist in water and soil due to slow degradation processes, which aggravate environmental pollution problems and are hard to eliminated by natural processes [5]. 4-BP has been listed as one of the priority toxic halogenated phenols by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) due to their genotoxic, mutagenic, and carcinogenic nature in the environment [6]. The advanced toxicological effects of BPs are solely dependent on their pattern and degree of debromination [7]. It has been investigated that bromine occurrence in nature influences the degradation efficiency and also assists the generation of toxic brominated by-products [8]. The NBFRs are suspected of having multiple detrimental effects to living organisms due to accretion of BPs on the surface and sediments [9]. The unawareness and exploitation of 4-BP has led to severe contamination and resulted in the huge damage in the aquifer and human society. Thus, 4-BP, a typical NBFR, has been selected as a target pollutant for the current research to establish an effective method for complete mineralization from contaminated environments. Various physical, chemical, and biological treatment procedures have been practiced for the treatment of halophenols in wastewater [10,11]. Yazidi et al. [12] interpreted the adsorption mechanism of 4-BP on activated carbon with the adsorption energy of −14.3 kJ/mol, but restoration and further treatment may be a challenging. Furthermore, the microbial degradation process generates brominated substitute groups which enhances the aromatic ring severity, hence reduces the efficacy of autogenerated microorganisms [13]. Therefore, various transition-metals and graphene-based catalysts have been regarded as potential photocatalysts for the treatment of bromophenols material due to their low cost, high chemical stability, and enhanced photoinduced charge carrier separation (Table S1) [14,15]. Further disposal of incomplete pollutant degradation, metal leaching, and toxic sludge generation are the major drawbacks of metallic photocatalysts [16–18]. The poor response under visible light and the fast recombination of the electron-hole pairs which extremely renders the inefficient use of solar irradiation due to wide band gap of TiO2 (Rutile 3.0 eV and anatase-3.2 eV) [10,19]. Since enormous visible light accounts for about 43% of total solar spectrum, which is higher than the UV light (5% of solar light), due to which we evaluated the photocatalytic degradation under visible light irradiation [20]. Large scale production and implementation, material stability, and reusability under solar light are also the major concern for practical applications [21,22].

With the mounting interest in sunlight-driven photocatalytic degradation (for environmental pollution), the metal-free semiconducting materials have attracted much attention. Upon solar light exposure, electron-hole pairs counter with molecular oxygen and hydroxyl ions (OH−), results to the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) responsible for complete mineralization of pollutants. Polymeric ordered-graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, CN) has drawn the attention due to inexpensive, visible light driven material for various photocatalytic applications including CO2 reduction, oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), and complete mineralization of organic pollutants. In this regard, various heterogenous photocatalysts have been developed and reported with superior efficiency under solar light irradiation for water treatment [23–26]. Tahir et al. [27] tailored CN material to 1D structure and introduced it for effective oxygen reduction reaction, due to improved oxygen functional groups on the surface, reduced charge transfer resistance, which provides more active sites on the surface. Furthermore, Luo et al. [28] demonstrated non-radical oxidation process with peroxymonosulphate (PMS) on carbon nitride for the degradation of bisphenol A. Despite numerous unique properties, bulk carbon nitride demonstrated poor photocatalytic performance under visible light due to high recombination rate (e
—
— h
+ pair) and low surface area (fewer active sites), thus hindering practical applications of such under-rated photocatalyst [29]. Hence, surface defect engineering and induction of quantum dots with CN–B have demonstrated higher visible light and long-wavelength light absorption, which promotes the separation of photogenerated e
—
— h
+ pairs and more reactive oxygen species [30–32]. In this regard, chemical exfoliation and in-plane functionalization of CN has been proven to be an effective method to improve the visible light adsorption capacity for enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [33]. Li et al. [34] synthesized an ultrathin 2D-CN/MoS2 heterojunction catalyst to improve the electronic mobility of the interface to attain high photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB.

Herein, the study reveals the synthesis of metal free in-plane functionalized CN@GQD composite by the induction of GQD into the layers and edges of the CN via hydrothermal process. As-synthesized CN–B, CN-Ex, and CN@GQD have been applied for the complete mineralization of 4-BP under LED visible light (λ = 420 nm) irradiation at room temperature and compared. The synergistic effect of GQD and CN has markedly facilitated the red-shift (improved band gap) and ample solar light (visible light) adsorption leading to complete mineralization of 4-BP. The objectives of the study were to leverage the features of CN@GQD to conquer the enhanced photo-induced complete mineralization of phenolic compounds. The stability and recyclability of the material was evaluated using various reaction conditions and 5 minimum repetitive cycles for 4-BP degradation. The photocatalytic degradation reaction mechanism of 4-BP was elucidated through identification of final products by using total organic carbon (TOC) analysis. The recycling properties without substantial loss and environmental benign nature shows a great potential to be an excellent metal-free material for treatment of micropollutants in the aquatic environment.

Urea (99% purity) was acquired from Alfa Aesar and 4-BP (99.8%) from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium perchlorate (NaClO) Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), potassium hydroxide (KOH), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Merck Co. Double distilled ultrapure water (ddW) was utilized for photocatalytic experiments, acquired from Milli-Q water system with 18.2 μs cm resistivity. All other required chemicals purchased and used for the synthesis and photocatalysis experiments, were of analytical grade.

GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid according to a protocol recently reported elsewhere [35]. Briefly, 5 g of citric acid was added into 200 mL round bottom flask and raised the temperature to 200 °C. At first stage, citric acid crystals turned from solid to colorless liquid and later gradually turned to pale orange. After constant heating for 30 min, the dark yellow colored liquid was collected followed by dropwise addition into 100 mL of 0.2 M KOH solution under continuous stirring to prepare homogenous solution. The obtained GQD solution was transferred in to 1 kDa dialysis tubing bag and purified by exchanging very small molecular solutes from DI water. The obtained neutral GQD solution was substantiated by UV light exposure and stored in a cool place for further use (scheme 1 a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of bulk and exfoliated graphitic carbon nitride
ce:section.ce:para: The bulk CN was synthesized with slight modification under high temperature calcination of urea, as described previously [36,37]. 10 g of Urea, 100 mg KOH, and 3 mL H2O2 (30%) were mixed homogeneously under sonication for 4 h, dried and calcined at 550 °C for 4 h at 5 °C min−1 inside the muffle furnace. The produced pale-yellow colored bulk carbon nitride (CN–B) material was crushed with a pestle, washed with ultrapure water, centrifuged and dried overnight at 80 °C. Furthermore, 2 M NaClO (60 mL) and 1 M HNO3 (15 mL) was added in 1.0 g CN–B, placed in the sonicator for 2 h to exfoliate CN–B. Afterward, the mixture was transferred to Teflon lining autoclave tubes and kept for constant heating for 5 h at 130 °C. The final mixture was then rinsed with ultrapure water to neutral pH and kept overnight at 80 °C. The final materials were labelled (CN-Ex) and further utilized for the synthesis of CN@GQD and photocatalytic applications.

1.0 g of CN-Ex was further dispersed in 50 mL DI water and ultrasonicated for 60 min to delaminated and generate uniform suspension of CN-Ex. Desired volume of aqueous GQD solution was added in the CN-Ex solution and kept for extended ultrasonication [38]. The obtained mixture was then transferred in Teflon lining autoclave tube and kept for 2 h constant heating at 120 °C (scheme 1b). The final product was allowed to cool down to room temperature and filtered through a filter paper. The resultant filtrate was dried overnight at 80 °C and kept in the dry place for further characterization and photocatalytic experiments.

The morphology of as-synthesized CN–B, CN-Ex, and CN@GQD was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high resolution TEM, and scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy dispersive X-ray (EDX). The microstructural and elemental distribution of C, N, and O in CN@GQD was analyzed by electron probe microanalyzer (EPMA, JEOL JXA-8200) with an acceleration voltage of 20 kV and 20 nA beam current. Furthermore, the crystal structures of the as-prepared photocatalysts were analyzed by using Bruker D8 Advanced X-ray diffractometer (XRD), Ni filtered Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5405 Å, at 40 mA current density and 40 kV voltage). The surface chemistry of as synthesized CN–B, CN-Ex, and CN@GQD were analyzed by synchrotron radiation based infrared micro-spectroscopy (SR-IMS, beamline-14 A, National Synchrotron Radiation Research Center (NSRRC), Hsinchu, Taiwan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS-ESCA PHI 1600 (Physical Electronics, Eden Prairie, MN, Al-Kα X-1486.6 eV photon energy). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area (SSA) and pore size distribution (Barrett, Joyner, and Halenda's, (BJH) models) were calculated by nitrogen adsorption and desorption at 77 K using ASAP 2020. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) was measured using the Hitachi U4100 UV–Visible Spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 200–800 nm.

The photocatalytic degradation of 4-BP was carried out in a glass reactor with a maximum capacity of 25 mL. In detail, the typical photocatalytic reactions were performed using 500 mg/L photocatalysts dose, 5 ppm of 4-BP as a targeted pollutant into the reactor under visible light. The mixture was sonicated for 1 min and kept in the dark for 30 min under continuous stirring to ensure the adsorption capacity of the material. The reaction mixture was then exposed to 150 W LED lamp (λ = 420 nm) light irradiation and 1 mL sample aliquots were collected from the reactor at regular intervals until complete mineralization of 4-BP. The reaction conditions, concentrations of catalyst and pollutant were maintained during the reaction. The initial concentration of the pollutant (5 ppm), catalysts (500 mg L-1), pH 6.8, and reaction temperature were used constantly during the entire experiment, until unless mentioned. The periodically withdrawn sample aliquots were immediately filtered through Millipore filter (0.22 μm). The resulting solution was analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) assembled with variable wavelength detector, at 270 nm absorption wavelength (λmax ). An equilibrated mixture of ∼3% acetic acid in methanol and water (70:30) at a fixed flow rate of 0.6 mL/min was used for HPLC method. The analytical precision of HPLC method was maintained within 3% by relative standard deviation during the entire analysis. All the experiments were performed in triplicate to ensure the possible error in handling and catalyst efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EPR measurement of CN@GQD induced ROS generation
ce:section.ce:para: The EPR spectra of the sample was recorded at room temperature by EMXplus-10/12/P/L spectrometer from Bruker using following instrumental settings: resonance frequency 9.82 GHz, modulation frequency 100 kHz, modulation amplitude: 1.0 G, sweep width: 200 G, time constant: 40.96 ms, sweep Time: 83.88 s, receiver Gain: 30 db. As-synthesized CN–B and CN@GQD composites were mixed with TEMP (20 mM) solution and recorded for EPR measurement under dark and visible light. TEMP solution was also analyzed without adding any catalysts, as a blank, under similar conditions. Furthermore, the production of superoxide (O2•¯) and hydroxyl (•OH) radicals were investigated using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), a spin trap agent.

The morphological characterizations of as-synthesized metal free catalysts were performed by microscopic analysis to reveal the modifications in its morphology. Fig. 1 a shows the physical morphology of CN–B with bulk and smooth structures on the surface. In addition, carbon nitride sheets are assembled, stacked, and densely aggregated due to high temperature polymerization. In case of CN@GQD (Fig. 1b), the curled flake-like structures and thinner plates on the surface resulting from the advanced oxidation and in-plane GQD impregnation, suggests well defined exfoliation of CN–B. CN@GQD further characterized by TEM (Fig. 1c) and HR-TEM (Fig. 1d) to reveal the exfoliation effect due to hydrothermal oxidation and GQD treatment. The TEM image of the material corroborates with HR-TEM and SEM results, with less dense and platelet-like layered structure, indicating the modification of the CN–B with GQD significantly enhances the textural properties of polymeric carbon nitride. Fig. S1 shows the thickness of the ultra-thin tubular carbon nitride nanosheets was around 0.9 nm and the size distribution of GQD on the surface of CN was in the range of 1–4 nm size. Li et al. [39] have demonstrated the modulation of ultra-thin tubular carbon nitride (∼8 nm) with carbon quantum dots with boosted visible light adsorption. Furthermore, accurate and precise elemental quantification was performed by EPMA (Fig. 1e–g), indicating the homogenous distribution of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in CN@GQD composite. The low oxygen concentration in the composite may be due to in-plane impregnation of GQD, containing numerous oxygen containing functional groups. The development of functionalized CN@GQD has been further characterized by Synchrotron X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to reveal the influence of oxygen containing groups in the composite.

XAS analysis was adopted to investigate the atomic structure and local electronic configuration of CN–B, CN-Ex, and CN@GQD. The K-edge XAS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s have been recorded clearly and compared in Fig. 2 a-c. The C 1s K-edge XAS spectra consists of strong 1s → π∗ (283–289.1 eV) and 1s → σ∗ (289.1–296 eV) band structure in case of CN@GQD, indicates the existence of graphitic structure due to in-plane impregnation of GQD within the composite [40,41]. CN–B, CN-Ex, and CN@GQD individually show structural defects at 284.8 eV and 287.5 eV due to significant interlayer CC bonding [42]. It is presumed that the intensity of π∗ (284.8 eV) in CN@GQD composites has been enhanced after GQD impregnation due to in-plane π bond delocalization, in comparison to CN–B and CN-Ex. The N 1s K-edge XAS spectra (Fig. 2b), four major and significant peaks have been observed at 400.6, 403.4, 406.4, and 409.4 eV corresponding to π∗ pyridine like double bond (NC), CN–C (graphitic nitrogen), N–3C (bridging among the tri-s-triazine moieties), and N–C (1s → σ∗) transition, respectively [43,44]. The interlayer C–N bonds are clearly detected in CN@GQD, which facilitate the connectivity between GQD and CN parallel layers for rapid electron transport during photo induced catalytic degradation of 4-BP [45]. It was noticed that the binding energies for peaks in C 1s and N 1s shifted to a lower value, which reflects the interfacial interaction between the two semiconductors. The findings suggest the conservation of the tri-s-triazine structure of basic carbon nitride, promoting the photogenerated electron transfer in CN@GQD to magnify its photocatalytic activity, which was also confirmed by FTIR analysis. The O 1s K-edge XAS analysis further reveals several surface modifications after annealing and hydrothermal treatment with GQD. Fig. 2c shows strong transition 1s → π∗ (533.6 eV) C–O bonding in the epoxide structure, which is decreased after hydrothermal treatment. Moreover, 1s → σ∗ (539.2, 544.1 eV for C–O–H, and O–C, respectively) transition peak, significantly evolved in CN@GQD composite, is probably the replacement of nitrogen atoms from oxygen. This enhancement in oxygen defect is likely to prolong the lifetime of photogenerated electrons [46].

Key parameters of photocatalytic performance can be described by the optical and emission properties of the photocatalyst using UV–Vis spectroscopy characterizations. In Fig. 3 a, the coupling of GQD and CN caused a substantial red shift in the optical absorption edge and subsequently improved the visible light (λ = 420 nm) exploiting capability of the composite compared to CN–B. Band gap narrowing was apparent after doping and anchoring modifications, which could allow the material to cover maximum solar photon flux and promote the enhanced photocatalytic degradation under visible light [47]. The band gap energy was calculated from the optical absorption near the band edge from Eq. (1),
(1)
(
a h v
)
n
= h v −
E g
where α is the absorption coefficient, hυ is absorption energy, n is the electronic transition of a semiconductor, and E
g is the band gap energy. The narrowing band gap was recorded which help to photoexcite the electrons with insignificant photon energy and separate into electron and hole pairs. Wang et al. [48] functionalized graphitic carbon nitride with oxygen element to influence the band position. Thus, in-plane and interfacial charge transfer can be controlled by the induction of foreign element, which enhanced the photocatalytic activity of the material.

The chemical structure, composition, and elemental states of the material have been further characterized XRD and FTIR. The crystal patterns of various carbon nitride materials are shown in Fig. 3b. The CN–B displays two characteristic diffraction peaks at 13.4° and 27.7°, indexed as (100) and (002) in-plane packing and interlayer stacking of tetragonal g-C3N4 (JCPDS no. 010781747) [49]. Moreover, the intensity of the peaks was considerably weakened due to the reduced crystallinity and the introduction of lattice defects by further heating and hydrothermal treatment in CN-Ex and CN@GQD. The as-prepared CN@GQD shows an additional broad peak at 25.1° and impurity peaks at 32.3°, ascribed to in-plane stacking of GQD [50]. The FTIR spectrum demonstrated multiple transmittance peaks in 1100–1600 cm−1 range, attributed to the aromatic C–N stretching and CN stretching vibrations of aromatic ring [51]. The prominent peaks at 806 and 885 cm−1 are associated to the bending and stretching vibrations of the s-triazine ring system (Fig. 3c). The large absorption band at 3000–3500 cm−1 is due to N–H, confirmed the presence of dangling hydrogens in the C–N layers of graphitic carbon nitride. It is expected for a broad peak to appear at 670-900 cm−1 after in-plane GQD impregnation due to C–O stretching vibrations of [52]. The vibration peaks obtained using infrared spectroscopy corroborates the results obtained in XAS analyses. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots of as-synthesized materials have been performed to investigate the electronic properties (Fig. 3d). CN@GQD was recorded with decreased diameter and straighten low frequency region compared to CN–B, suggesting more efficient electron transfer and charge separation efficacy [53]. Exfoliation and modifications of CN–B have been well defined and characterized by SSA and BJH pore size distribution (Fig. 3e and f). As-synthesized materials shows type IV isotherm and H3 hysteresis, signifying the capillary condensation in mesopores and micropores. The specific surface area of CN@GQD has been enhanced more than 2-folds (21.62–59.30 m2/g) after hydrothermal treatment in the presence of GQD. The enlarged surface areas and improved micro pores in CN@GQD may be due to the perfect exfoliation and GQD immobilization on pristine CN–B, which could provide more active sites. Wang et al. [53] reported the addition of oxygen elements in the pristine carbon nitride framework, which helps to narrow the band gap. Exfoliation and addition of oxygen containing functional groups are anticipated to temper the band-gap structure to intensify the electron-hole separation efficacy of the CN@GQD. Hasija et al. [17] also demonstrated the induction of carbon quantum dots to construct heterojunction by immobilizing bimetallic nanocomposite and CN, which could extend the visible light adsorption and synergistic process helping to mineralize 2,4-dinitrophenol.

The as-synthesized nano-composites have been applied to evaluate the photocatalytic efficacy towards the mineralization of 4-BP, a NBFRs as targeted pollutant in aqueous solution under controlled visible irradiation. The catalyst was dispersed in 4-BP solution and stirred in the dark to ensure that the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was attained before light irradiation. No abatement of 4-BP was observed when the reaction was performed in the absence of catalysts under 420 nm LED light irradiation for 90 min. Despite the fact that carbon-based materials are known to be excellent adsorbents of micropollutants, only 4–8% of 4-BP were removed by CN–B, CN-EX, and CN@GQD after 90 min of continuous mixing (Fig. 4 a). Furthermore, 4-BP degradation that took place immediately after visible light irradiation, confirmed that 4-BP elimination is due to photocatalysis and not to adsorption. Pristine CN–B shows 6% adsorption under dark condition, while upon light irradiation degradation, the reaction reached to 76.66% of 4-BP. The degradation of 4-BP has been anticipated to follow pseudo-first-order rate kinetics (Eq. (2)), owing the large number of catalyst and constant 4-BP concentration [36,54,55]:
(2)

CN-Ex demonstrated almost complete degradation in 90 min of reaction time, which may be due to large surface area in comparison to CN–B. Guo et al. [56] reported that the exfoliation of carbon nitride into nanosheets can suppress the recombination of photogenerated carriers, resulting in superior photocatalytic performance. Consistent with the results of the material characterization, 4-BP removal by the as-prepared heterojunction outperformed than that of bulk-CN. The CN@GQD was recorded with highest photocatalytic performance. Nearly 4-fold of 4-BP rate was achieved with CN@GQD in 60 min of visible light irradiation, probably due to large surface area and high electron transfer, while blank solutions still remain constant during the reactions. Hou et al. [57] demonstrated the novel g-C3N4 with tunable nitrogen effectively degrades 4-chlorophenol (81% TOC removal), wherein OH● radicals and holes were the major oxygen reactive species for the removal. Similarly, Li et al. [58] synthesized controlled carbon quantum dot/CN nano-frames with heterogeneous interfaces within two-dimensional in-plane and out-of-plane domains for remarkable visible light active material for micropollutant treatment. The controlled introduction of lattice defects improves the photocatalytic performance while suppressing the recombination of excitons from enlarged area for surface charge. The addition of GQD in this study provides higher surface area and light adsorption properties with advanced reactive sites. Fig. 4b shows the TOC removal and released Br-concentration after 60 min of visible light exposure. More than 88% TOC was achieved with approx. 0.4 ppm Br− in the solution, suggesting almost complete mineralization of 4-BP as compared to some controls (CN@GQD only, 4-BP only, 4-BP and CN@GQD at time 0 min). The control solutions (without catalysts) remain constant after 2 h LED visible light exposure, indicating the stable chemical structure under visible light irradiation. In order to check the stability and recyclability of the photocatalyst, a regeneration experiment was conducted shown in Fig. 4c. CN@GQD was irradiated under controlled LED visible light for consecutive runs using the same photocatalyst each time. The concentration of both pollutant and photocatalyst were maintained in accordance with previous runs. After five cycles of repeated photocatalyst usage, minimal decrease in 4-BP removal was noted due to loss of trace amounts of photocatalysts during aliquots filtration and the accretion of intermediates onto the photocatalyst surface which competes with 4-BP molecules. Subsequently, TOC was calculated after each photocatalytic cycle and the numerical values are 88.76% @1st cycle >82.73% @2nd cycle >73.97% @3rd cycle >65.23% @4th cycle >62.73% @5th cycle, which is quite consistent with rate of 4-BP. Slight decrease in apparent degradation efficiency of 4-BP was recorded after 5 consecutive cycles, which may be due to the accumulation of some byproducts which could reduce the proportion of active sites on CN@GQD. The recycled CN@GQD material was further characterized by FTIR and XRD to confirm the surface modifications and crystallinity. Peaks at 1240 and 1560 cm−1 appeared due to further exfoliation of carbon nitride layers corresponding to C–N and CN, respectively (Fig. S2) [59]. Fig. S3 shows the XRD peak centered at 27.5° (002), attributed to in-plane tri-s-triazine packing of used CN@GQD material. Despite the recycling of the material, the chemical structure of the material remained unchanged. Similar phenomenon was observed by Cui et al. [60], which indicated the retained physico-chemical properties of mesoporous carbon nitride after repeated photocatalytic degradation of 4-chlorophenol.

In consideration for industrial application, optimization of the catalyst dosage is regarded vital, economy wise. Experimental investigations were conducted to observe the degradation efficiency of 4-BP under various catalyst dosage. A linear behavior of the photodegradation rate was observed and undoubtedly followed the pseudo first order kinetics. Fig. 5 shows the influence of different carbon nitride catalysts, catalysts dose, initial pH, and 4-BP concentration and compared with the rate obtained. As-synthesized CN@GQD demonstrated remarkably increased rate of 4-BP in comparison to CN–B. In addition, when the catalyst loading changed from 200 mg/L to 2.0 g L−1, the destruction rate of 4-BP was systematically enhanced. The analytical values of k
obs are organized in the succeeding order: 0 g L−1 catalysts-0.000 min−1; 0.200 g L−1 catalysts-0.0219 min−1; 0.50 g L−1 catalysts-0.060 min−1; 1.0 g L−1 catalysts-0.073 min−1; 2.0 g L−1 catalysts-0.0817 min−1. Increasing the catalysts dose from 500 mg/L to 2.0 g L−1 does not affect the rate too much, due to enhanced opaqueness of the reaction solution (reduced proton permeability) and particle integration (reduced active sites) [61]. Thus, catalytic dosage was not enhanced further to avoid expensive operating cost with lower light utilization efficiency, leading towards inefficiency in excitation and separation of electron and hole pairs hence, achieving slower 4-BP removal [52,56]. Furthermore, the versatile photocatalytic properties of CN@GQD at different pH were also monitored, ranging from 4.8 to 9.2 pH scale. The rate of 4-BP decreases with increase in pH (pH 4.8–0.0612 min−1; pH 6.8–0.060 min−1; and pH 9.2–0.0612 min−1), which is similar to the removal kinetics of 4-BP in a metal catalytic system [62]. The influence of 4-BP mineralization at high pH could result from the generation and distribution of oxidative species and also the charge over CN@GQD surface during photocatalysis reaction under visible light [63]. The high pKa value (9.15) of 4-BP, indicating the anionic form of the pollutant, do not allow to be strongly adsorbed on the catalyst surface, hence decreases the rate of 4-BP degradation [6,64]. The acidic conditions favor the 4-BP degradation due to the fact that protonated nitrogen species induces the positive charge on the catalysts surface to facilitate the pollutant adsorption and mineralization [60]. The effect of initial 4-BP concentration in the reaction was evaluated from 2.5 ppm to 10 ppm. The results clearly indicate that the increasing initial 4-BP concentration significantly reduces the degradation efficiency of the material. The limited reactive oxygen species at constant catalyst dose, visible light source, and time of reaction are inadequate to mineralize 4-BP completely, which is similar to the removal of phenol by metal oxide/CN catalysts [65].

ce:section.ce:section-title: EPR measurement of ROS production and proposed reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para:
The electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap technique was used to identify the formation of ROS, presented in Fig. 6 . The formation of singlet oxygen (1O2) species were investigated with a spin trap 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP). The radical trapping reagent (TEMP) was first analyzed under dark and then exposed to visible light for 30 min, without adding any catalysts to confirm the interference. Further, as-synthesized metal-free composites were mixed homogenously in 20 mM TEMP trapping reagent and EPR spectra was recorded at regular intervals for 30 min. Fig. 6a shows small exciting pulses of 1O2, during standard experiment under dark in all the samples, due to short lifetime (2 μS) in H2O solvent [66]. As expected, TEMP molecules quickly react with 1O2 and form TEMP-1O2, a stable EPR active product, when exposed to constant visible light (λ = 420 nm) [67]. The obtained results suggest the slow increase in characteristic TEMPO signal intensity in 10 min (Fig. 6b), while significantly higher intensity was recorded after 30 min exposure (Fig. 6c). In contrast, the CN@GQD shows strong signal intensity than CN–B, suggesting stable and higher 1O2 generation in the reaction with increased light exposure time. Additionally, O2 ● ̶ and ●OH radical production was investigated using DMPO spin trap under dark and visible light exposure. Fig. 6d does not show the characteristics peak intensity of EPR signals under dark, while after 30 min light exposure induces very low intensity signals in CN@GQD composite. The recorded EPR results suggest the negligible amount of O2 ● ̶ and ●OH production during photo-degradation of 4-BP and entire degradation reaction dominated by h
+ and 1O2 radicals. Scheme 2 shows the plausible photocatalysis mechanism of 4-BP associated with CN@GQD catalyst, considering other factors that decontrol the photocatalytic mechanism. As per the above discussion, the enhanced photocatalytic mechanism of CN@GQD toward 4-BP degradation has been elucidated. The introduction of GQD for in-plane functionalization of CN, influenced the band-gap and red shift of their light adsorption, resulting in the higher production of 1O2 radicals. Katsumata et al. [51] demonstrated O-doping into the CN framework to modulate the energy band-gap and photoinduced charge carriers, which generate both h
+ and O2 ● ̶ as predominant oxidizing species. However, Guo et al. [56] revealed the significant role of photogenerated electrons from g-C3N4 to generate O2 ● ̶ and 1O2 under visible light irradiation. The TOC data obtained after nearly complete mineralization of 4-BP demonstrated the scissoring mechanism in the phenolic ring opening, and Br-ion concentration detected by IC, confirmed the conversion of 4-BP into nontoxic CO2 and H2O. Therefore, this metal free CN@GQD catalyst exhibits an excellent photocatalytic mineralization of 4-BP under abundant visible light irradiation.

The CN@GQD metal free composite was successfully synthesized via high temperature calcination, followed by hydrothermal treatment. The highly exfoliated CN generates new impurity levels within, and the GQD doping helped to extend the visible–light absorption efficiency and lowered bandgap. The physical and electrochemical characterization including STEM, XAS, FTIR, and XRD were performed to confirm the surface modification. The bandgap energy of CN–B, CN-Ex, and CN@GQD was explored through DRS. The EIS analysis demonstrated that the superior conductivity was generated to reconstruct electrons from VB, and supported GQD atoms generate an electron field to enhance the reductive efficiency on CB. Metal-free CN@GQD composite has shown more efficient and consistent photocatalytic degradation of 4-BP, which might debrominated and oxidized on CB and VB, respectively. The CB generates electrons for debromination followed by oxidation and mineralization procedures initiated by 1O2 radicals. The CN@GQD composite exhibited 4-folds (0.015–0.060 min−1) photocatalytic activity over CN–B. Moreover, excellent stability and TOC removal rate was observed up to 5 consecutive cycles with 0.0296 min−1 4-BP rate. Various reaction conditions were also optimized to explore the feasibility and wide range applicability of the material. This research provides flexible synthesis procedure to design an efficient and stable photocatalyst by hybridizing GQD and carbon nitride to modify the band gap, enhance hydrophilicity and photocatalytic activity for persistent organic pollutant degradation under visible–light.

Rama Shanker Sahu: Experimental Performance, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Abhishek Dubey: Experimental Performance for Revision, Data curation. Yang-hsin Shih: Conceptualization, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
